title: Turbo-folk

infobox.value: * [Serbian folk music](/wiki/Serbian_folk_music "Serbian folk music") * [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") * [Eurodance](/wiki/Eurodance "Eurodance") * [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno")

([Serbian Cyrillic](/wiki/Serbian_Cyrillic_alphabet "Serbian Cyrillic alphabet"): турбо-фолк) is a subgenre of contemporary [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs") pop music that initially developed in [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") during the 1990s as a fusion of [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno") and [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"). The term "turbo-folk" was coined by [Montenegrin](/wiki/Montenegrins "Montenegrins") singer [Rambo Amadeus](/wiki/Rambo_Amadeus "Rambo Amadeus"), who jokingly used it to describe an aggressive, satirical style of music.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) While primarily associated with Serbia, this genre is also popular in other former Yugoslav nations, particularly [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") and [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina").

[Rambo Amadeus](https://eurovision.tv/participant/rambo-amadeus), *eurovision.tv*, [2012 Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/2012_Eurovision_Song_Contest "2012 Eurovision Song Contest") participant profile

Stakić, Vladimir (11 November 1988). ["Ogledalo tamne budućnosti"](https://istorijskenovine.unilib.rs/view/index.html#panel:pp%7Cissue:UB_00064_19881111%7Carticle:article866%7Cpage:9%7C). *Борба*. No. 316. p. 9. "Uostalom, već samo njegovo umetničko ime to govori, kao i njegov termin za muzički pravac kojim se bavi — „turbo folk"." [Besides, this is already shown by his artistic alias, as well as by his name for the musical style that he works in – "turbo folk".]

Cvoro, Urosh (2016). *Turbo-folk Music and Cultural Representations of National Identity in Former Yugoslavia*. Taylor and Francis. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1317006060](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1317006060 "Special:BookSources/978-1317006060").
Marina Radoš, [Narodnjaci, ćirilica i turbofolk: Što su to uopće cajke?](https://www.index.hr/magazin/clanak/narodnjaci-cirilica-i-turbofolk-sto-su-to-uopce-cajke/2440998.aspx)

["Croatia Songs (Week of February 19, 2022)"](https://www.billboard.com/charts/croatia-songs-hotw/2022-02-19/). *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")*. 15 February 2022. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20220217114748/https://www.billboard.com/charts/croatia-songs-hotw/2022-02-19/) from the original on 17 February 2022. Retrieved 17 February 2022.

Marjanović, Hrvoje (18 February 2022). ["Billboard Croatia nikad neće biti Билборд Кроејша"](https://www.index.hr/clanak.aspx?id=2340703). *[Index.hr](/wiki/Index.hr "Index.hr")* (in Croatian). Retrieved 20 February 2022.

Müller-Suleymanova, Dilyara (2023). ["Shadows of the past : violent conflict and its repercussions for second-generation Bosnians in the diaspora"](https://digitalcollection.zhaw.ch/handle/11475/23217?pk_vid=afcc797701003c4a172241141544700b). *Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies*. **49** (7): 1786–1802. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1080/1369183X.2021.1973392](https://doi.org/10.1080%2F1369183X.2021.1973392). [hdl](/wiki/Hdl_(identifier) "Hdl (identifier)"):[11475/23217](https://hdl.handle.net/11475%2F23217).
["In These Times 25/07 -- Serbia's New New Wave"](http://www.inthesetimes.com/issue/25/07/hockenos2507.html). *Inthesetimes.com*. Retrieved 23 April 2017.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Music genre from Serbia

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **has an unclear [citation style](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Citation_style "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. The references used may be made clearer with a different or consistent style of [citation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") and [footnoting](/wiki/Help:Footnotes "Help:Footnotes"). *(October 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

| Turbo-folk | |
| --- | --- |
| Stylistic origins | * [Serbian folk music](/wiki/Serbian_folk_music "Serbian folk music") * [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") * [Eurodance](/wiki/Eurodance "Eurodance") * [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno") |
| Cultural origins | [1980s](/wiki/1980s_in_music "1980s in music") [SFR Yugoslavia](/wiki/SFR_Yugoslavia "SFR Yugoslavia") |
| Typical instruments | * [Accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion") * [brass instruments](/wiki/Brass_instruments "Brass instruments") * [synthesizer](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer") |
| Fusion genres | |
| [Pop-folk](/wiki/Folk-pop "Folk-pop") | |
| Other topics | |
| * [Chalga](/wiki/Chalga "Chalga") * [laïkó](/wiki/La%C3%AFk%C3%B3 "Laïkó") * [manele](/wiki/Manele "Manele") * [tallava](/wiki/Tallava "Tallava") | |

**Turbo-folk** ([Serbian Cyrillic](/wiki/Serbian_Cyrillic_alphabet "Serbian Cyrillic alphabet"): турбо-фолк) is a subgenre of contemporary [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs") pop music that initially developed in [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") during the 1990s as a fusion of [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno") and [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"). The term "turbo-folk" was coined by [Montenegrin](/wiki/Montenegrins "Montenegrins") singer [Rambo Amadeus](/wiki/Rambo_Amadeus "Rambo Amadeus"), who jokingly used it to describe an aggressive, satirical style of music.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) While primarily associated with Serbia, this genre is also popular in other former Yugoslav nations, particularly [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") and [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina").

## Croatia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turbo-folk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Croatia")]

Turbo-folk grew in Croatia in part due to the popularity of the Croatian singer [Severina](/wiki/Severina_(singer) "Severina (singer)")'s style of fusion music. Turbo-folk is purportedly seen as a "part of everyday life in Croatia and serves a means of social release and reaction to the effects of globalisation in Croatia" according to [contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art") professor Urosh Cvoro of [UNSW Sydney](/wiki/UNSW_Sydney "UNSW Sydney").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Upon introduction of [*Billboard* Croatia Songs](/wiki/Croatia_Songs "Croatia Songs") chart on 15 February 2022, it became apparent that mainstream music from Serbia and other former Yugoslav republics (which is all described as turbo-folk or by a derogatory term "*cajka*" (plural: *cajke*) by its critics in Croatia)[[4]](#cite_note-4) dominated the music taste of the people of Croatia, as the only Croatian artists featured on the chart were [Eni Jurišić](/wiki/Eni_Juri%C5%A1i%C4%87 "Eni Jurišić"), [Matija Cvek](/wiki/Matija_Cvek "Matija Cvek"), [30zona](https://hr.wikipedia.org/wiki/30zona "hr:30zona"), [Kuku$ Klan](https://hr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kuku$_Klan "hr:Kuku$ Klan"), [Jelena Rozga](/wiki/Jelena_Rozga "Jelena Rozga") and [Grše](/wiki/Gr%C5%A1e "Grše"), and the only Western artists featured on the chart were [Glass Animals](/wiki/Glass_Animals "Glass Animals") and [Red Hot Chili Peppers](/wiki/Red_Hot_Chili_Peppers "Red Hot Chili Peppers").[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Central Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turbo-folk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Central Europe")]

Turbo-folk can be heard in Balkan clubs and Ex-Yu-style discos in parts of [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") that speak German. Reports of turbo-folk from 2023 describe the music used for diasporic youth in these areas to "socialise and live out the culture of their country of origin" according to Dr Müller-Suleymanova of [ZHAW](/wiki/Zurich_University_of_Applied_Sciences/ZHAW "Zurich University of Applied Sciences/ZHAW").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turbo-folk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Criticism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Grafit_protiv_turbofolka%2CImotski01848.JPG/250px-Grafit_protiv_turbofolka%2CImotski01848.JPG)](/wiki/File:Grafit_protiv_turbofolka,Imotski01848.JPG)

Graffiti against [Ceca](/wiki/Ceca_(singer) "Ceca (singer)")'s music in [Imotski](/wiki/Imotski "Imotski"), [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"): "Turn off the 'Cecas'/Light up the candles/[Vukovar](/wiki/Vukovar_massacre "Vukovar massacre") will never/Be forgotten" (written with [stylized U's](/wiki/Usta%C5%A1e#Symbols "Ustaše") symbolizing the [Croatian nationalist](/wiki/Croatian_nationalist "Croatian nationalist") and fascist organisation [Ustaše](/wiki/Usta%C5%A1e "Ustaše"))

Critics of turbo-folk alleged that it was a promotional instrument of Serbia's political ideology during [Milošević](/wiki/Milo%C5%A1evi%C4%87 "Milošević") rule.[[8]](#cite_note-Hockenos2001-8) This liberal section of Serbian and Croatian society explicitly viewed this music as vulgar, almost pornographic [kitsch](/wiki/Kitsch "Kitsch"), glorifying crime, moral corruption and [nationalist](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") [xenophobia](/wiki/Xenophobia "Xenophobia"). In addition to making a connection between turbofolk and "[war profiteering](/wiki/War_profiteering "War profiteering"), crime & weapons cult, rule of force and violence", in her book *Smrtonosni sjaj* (Deadly Splendor) Belgrade media theorist Ivana Kronja refers to its look as "aggressive, sadistic and pornographically eroticised [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography")".[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-cirjak-10) Along the same lines, British culture theorist Alexei Monroe calls the phenomenon "porno-nationalism".[[11]](#cite_note-11) However, turbo-folk was equally popular amongst the [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs") peoples during the [Yugoslav Wars](/wiki/Yugoslav_Wars "Yugoslav Wars").[[10]](#cite_note-cirjak-10)

> As long as I am the mayor, there will be no nightclub-singers of [*cajke*] or turbo-folk parades in a single municipal hall.

— [Anto Đapić](/wiki/Anto_%C4%90api%C4%87 "Anto Đapić"), former mayor of [Osijek](/wiki/Osijek "Osijek") and leader of the [Croatian Party of Rights](/wiki/Croatian_Party_of_Rights "Croatian Party of Rights")[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The resilience of a turbo-folk culture and musical genre, often referred to as the *"soundtrack to Serbia’s wars"*,[[13]](#cite_note-13) was and to a certain extent still is, actively promoted and exploited by pro-government commercial TV stations, most notably on [Pink](/wiki/Pink_TV_(Serbia) "Pink TV (Serbia)") and [Palma](/wiki/RTV_Palma "RTV Palma") TV-channels, which devote significant amount of their broadcasting schedule to turbo-folk shows and [music videos](/wiki/Music_video "Music video").

Others, however, feel that this neglects the specific social and political context that brought about turbo-folk, which was, they say, entirely different from the context of contemporary western popular culture. In their opinion, turbo-folk served as a dominant paradigm of the "militant [nationalist](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism")" regime of [Slobodan Milošević](/wiki/Slobodan_Milo%C5%A1evi%C4%87 "Slobodan Milošević"), "fully controlled by regime media managers".[[14]](#cite_note-14) John Fiske feels that during that period, turbo-folk and its close counterpart, Serbian [Eurodance](/wiki/Eurodance "Eurodance"), had the monopoly over the officially permitted popular culture, while, according to him, in contrast, Western mass [media culture](/wiki/Media_culture "Media culture") of the time provided a variety of music genre, youth styles, and consequently [ideological](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology") positions.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
